title: Sample Data Integrity Policy
heading: Document Start
text: 1. Purpose This policy defines data integrity expectations for GMP records, computerized systems, laboratory instruments, and manufacturing documentation. The objective is to ensure that records are trustworthy, complete, accurate, and available throughout the required retention period. 2. Scope This policy applies to paper records, electronic records, hybrid records, spreadsheets, laboratory systems, manufacturing execution systems, audit trails, and electronic signatures used to support GMP decisions. 3. ALCOA+ Principles ALCOA+ means that data should be attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original, accurate, complete, consistent, enduring, and available. Each record must show who performed the activity, what was performed, when it was performed, and whether any correction or modification occurred. 4. Computerized System Controls Computerized systems used for GMP activities shall be validated according to intended use and risk. User access must be controlled using unique user IDs. Shared accounts are not permitted for GMP activities. Administrator privileges shall be limited and periodically reviewed. 5. Audit Trails Audit trails shall capture the creation, modification, deletion, and review of GMP data where technically possible. Audit trail review shall be performed based on risk and shall focus on critical data, unexpected changes, deleted results, repeated testing, and changes made outside normal process flow. 6. Metadata Metadata refers to contextual information required to understand a GMP record. Examples include user ID, timestamp, instrument ID, method version, sample ID, sequence number, file path, and processing parameters. Metadata must be retained with the primary record when it is required to reconstruct or verify the activity. 7. Data Review Data review shall include verification of completeness, accuracy, and consistency. Reviewers shall check for missing entries, unexplained changes, invalidated results, incomplete audit trail review, and inconsistency between source data and reported data. 8. Record Corrections Corrections to paper records must preserve the original entry. Electronic corrections must be traceable through system audit trails or equivalent controls. Any correction that may impact product quality, safety, identity, strength, purity, or compliance must be evaluated through the deviation or investigation process. 9. Backup and Retention Electronic GMP data must be backed up according to an approved backup schedule. Backup restoration shall be periodically tested. Records must remain readable and accessible throughout the retention period. 10. Exceptions If a system does not have audit trail capability, a documented risk assessment must define compensating controls. Compensating controls may include procedural review, restricted access, independent verification, or additional reconciliation.

title: Sample Gmp Sop
heading: Document Start
text: 1. Purpose This Standard Operating Procedure describes the minimum expectations for batch manufacturing, line clearance, deviation handling, and documentation control in a pharmaceutical manufacturing area. The procedure applies to operators, supervisors, quality assurance personnel, and engineering support staff involved in routine production activities. 2. Scope This SOP applies to non-sterile solid dose manufacturing activities, including dispensing, blending, compression, coating, packaging, and reconciliation. It also applies to supporting activities such as equipment cleaning, logbook completion, and controlled document usage. 3. Definitions Good Manufacturing Practice means the part of quality assurance that ensures products are consistently produced and controlled according to quality standards appropriate for their intended use. A batch record refers to the controlled document that captures all critical manufacturing instructions, checks, entries, signatures, and quality approvals for a batch. 4. Roles and Responsibilities 4.1 Production Operator The production operator shall perform the manufacturing step according to the approved batch record and current SOPs. The operator must verify equipment status, material identity, label details, and area cleanliness before starting the activity. The operator is responsible for recording entries contemporaneously and reporting abnormalities immediately. 4.2 Production Supervisor The production supervisor shall ensure that trained personnel are assigned to each operation. The supervisor must verify that line clearance is completed before batch start and that deviations are raised when unplanned events occur. The supervisor is responsible for reviewing batch record completeness before quality review. 4.3 Quality Assurance Quality assurance is responsible for independent verification of critical GMP activities. QA shall review deviations, approve batch record corrections, verify line clearance where required, and ensure that product disposition decisions are supported by documented evidence. 5. Procedure 5.1 Pre-Operation Checks Before the operation begins, the operator shall verify that the area is clean, free from previous product residues, and labelled with the correct status. Equipment must be checked for cleanliness, calibration status, and preventive maintenance status. Materials must be verified against the batch record, including material code, batch number, expiry date, and quantity. 5.2 Line Clearance Line clearance shall be performed before starting a new batch, product, or campaign. The check must confirm that previous product labels, printed packaging materials, loose tablets, containers, and documents have been removed. Any discrepancy found during line clearance must be escalated to the supervisor and QA before proceeding. 5.3 Manufacturing Execution The operator shall follow the approved sequence in the batch record. Critical process parameters such as mixing time, compression force, coating temperature, and in-process control frequency must be recorded as required. If actual values fall outside the approved range, the activity must be stopped and a deviation must be initiated. 5.4 Deviation Handling Any unplanned departure from an approved instruction, specification, or expected process condition shall be treated as a deviation. The operator must immediately inform the supervisor and QA. The supervisor shall secure the area, segregate affected materials if required, and record the initial event details. QA shall assess potential product quality impact and determine whether investigation, CAPA, or additional testing is required. 5.5 Documentation Practices All entries must be clear, accurate, legible, contemporaneous, original, and attributable. Corrections must be made by drawing a single line through the incorrect entry, entering the correct value, signing, dating, and providing a reason where required. Correction fluid, overwriting, backdating, and unsigned entries are not permitted. 6. Exceptions If equipment failure occurs during processing, the batch must be placed on hold until quality impact is assessed. If a required trained operator is unavailable, the activity must not proceed unless an alternate trained person is assigned and documented. 7. Records Completed batch records, equipment logbooks, line clearance checklists, cleaning records, and deviation reports shall be retained according to the site record retention schedule.

title: Sample Validation Guideline
heading: Document Start
text: 1. Purpose This guideline describes lifecycle expectations for equipment qualification, process validation, cleaning validation, and continued process verification in a GMP manufacturing environment. 2. Validation Lifecycle Validation is performed through a lifecycle approach that includes process design, qualification, process performance qualification, and continued process verification. The level of effort shall be based on product risk, process complexity, historical knowledge, and impact on patient safety. 3. Equipment Qualification Equipment qualification shall demonstrate that equipment is suitable for intended use. Qualification may include design qualification, installation qualification, operational qualification, and performance qualification. Critical instruments must be calibrated before qualification execution. 4. Process Validation Process validation means documented evidence that a process, operated within established parameters, can perform effectively and reproducibly to produce a medicinal product meeting predetermined specifications and quality attributes. 5. Process Performance Qualification Process performance qualification shall confirm that the commercial manufacturing process performs as expected under routine conditions. The protocol must define acceptance criteria, sampling plan, critical process parameters, critical quality attributes, batch selection rationale, and deviation handling requirements. 6. Continued Process Verification Continued process verification shall monitor process performance after validation approval. Process data shall be periodically reviewed to identify trends, variability, repeated deviations, and opportunities for control strategy improvement. 7. Cleaning Validation Cleaning validation shall demonstrate that cleaning procedures can remove product residues, cleaning agents, and microbial contamination to acceptable limits. Acceptance criteria shall be scientifically justified and based on toxicological, pharmacological, or quality risk considerations. 8. Change Control Changes to validated systems, equipment, processes, materials, or cleaning procedures shall be assessed through change control. The assessment must determine whether requalification, revalidation, additional testing, or document updates are required. 9. Deviations During Validation Any deviation during validation execution must be documented and assessed for impact on the validity of the study. A validation deviation may require repeat testing, protocol amendment, additional sampling, or CAPA depending on the root cause and quality impact. 10. Validation Report The validation report shall summarize executed activities, deviations, results, acceptance criteria, conclusions, and recommendations. The report must clearly state whether the process, equipment, or cleaning procedure is considered validated.
